topography.1: Steep slopes combined with Valencia's intense autumn rainfall events (DANA) create a high risk of erosion and soil loss. This makes the transition to regenerative practices harder but also more urgent.
topography.2: Moderate slopes carry moderate erosion risk. Good soil cover and water retention practices become especially valuable here to reduce runoff.
topography.3: Flat terrain minimises erosion risk and allows water to infiltrate naturally, creating a favourable starting point for regenerative practices.

pest_pressure.1: High pest and disease pressure in conventional systems often reflects low biodiversity and high synthetic input dependency. Transitioning away from pesticides takes longer under these conditions.
pest_pressure.2: Moderate pressure means regenerative practices can begin replacing inputs with manageable short-term risk — a realistic and achievable transition path.
pest_pressure.3: Low pest pressure signals an already-favourable ecosystem balance. Biological control can be established relatively quickly with targeted habitat improvements.

soil_salinity.1: Coastal salt accumulation limits what RA practices are feasible and requires careful drainage management before many improvements can take effect.
soil_salinity.2: Moderate salinity is manageable with good drainage and organic matter build-up, which improves soil structure and salt leaching.
soil_salinity.3: Low salinity removes a significant barrier, making a wide range of regenerative practices immediately accessible.

fruit_set.1: Poor fruit set below 15% indicates severe tree stress, often linked to malnutrition, poor root development or soil health problems — a key signal to address soil management urgently.
fruit_set.2: Normal fruit set of 15–45% is standard for citrus and indicates the tree is coping but may benefit from further soil health and nutrition improvements.
fruit_set.3: Excellent fruit set above 45% is an indicator of outstanding tree health, good soil nutrition and effective regenerative management — confirm with soil structure checks.

irrigation.1: Flood irrigation on a fixed schedule wastes 40–60% of water through runoff and evaporation in Valencia's climate, accelerating aquifer depletion and risking soil salinisation.
irrigation.2: Drip or sprinkler irrigation with semi-consistent scheduling reduces water use significantly compared to flood irrigation, but still misses precision opportunities.
irrigation.3: Adaptive drip irrigation based on actual crop and soil needs delivers water only when needed, minimising waste, reducing energy costs and preventing plant stress.

green_cover.1: Bare soil between rows leads to high runoff, low infiltration and increased aquifer dependency — the opposite of what regenerative water management requires.
green_cover.2: Moderate cover between rows reduces runoff and improves infiltration, beginning to close the water cycle at farm level.
green_cover.3: Dense, diverse year-round cover maximises infiltration, retains moisture in the root zone, reduces irrigation need and suppresses evaporation.

som_management.1: Without organic matter management, soil remains compacted with low water-holding capacity — wasting irrigation and increasing aquifer dependency.
som_management.2: Occasional compost or cover crop residues improve soil biology, and with them, water retention — a good first step toward regenerative water management.
som_management.3: Regular organic inputs and active SOM management can double or triple the soil's water-holding capacity, reducing irrigation needs and aquifer stress.

earthworms.1: Absence of earthworms is a clear signal of biologically depleted soil — typically caused by tillage, synthetic inputs or lack of organic matter. Earthworms are a key driver of soil structure and water infiltration.
earthworms.2: Two functional groups indicate recovery is underway. Endogeic and epigeic earthworms together signal improving organic matter and soil structure.
earthworms.3: All three functional groups — epigeic, endogeic and anecic — present at high density indicate a fully functioning soil food web, excellent organic matter cycling and strong biological activity.

auxiliary_fauna.1: Absence of ladybugs and beneficial insects signals a biological desert — often caused by pesticide use eliminating both pests and their natural predators.
auxiliary_fauna.2: Occasional sightings indicate some habitat exists but populations have not yet established consistently — targeted flower strips or habitat features can accelerate recovery.
auxiliary_fauna.3: Widespread, consistent presence of beneficial insects indicates a functioning biological control system reducing the need for any pest management inputs.

indicator_plants.1: Bare or monoculture inter-rows eliminate biological nitrogen fixation, ground-level habitat and the plant diversity that supports insect communities.
indicator_plants.2: Some plant diversity with limited legumes is a positive early signal, beginning to provide nitrogen fixation, food sources and habitat for soil organisms.
indicator_plants.3: High diversity with abundant leguminous plants provides biological nitrogen fixation (potentially 30–150 kg N/ha/year), habitat for pollinators and predators, and soil cover year-round.

livestock_integration.1: Without livestock, biomass can only be managed mechanically or chemically, missing the opportunity to convert on-farm vegetation into fertility through natural cycling.
livestock_integration.2: Partial integration — seasonal or zone-based grazing — begins to use livestock as a management tool, distributing fertility and reducing vegetation management costs.
livestock_integration.3: Livestock as the primary weed control mechanism eliminates herbicide and mowing costs, distributes fertility across the orchard and integrates animal production into the farm system.

tillage_type.1: Full inversion tillage (>30 cm) destroys soil structure, severs mycorrhizal networks, kills earthworms and fungal communities, releases stored carbon and creates compaction hardpan layers.
tillage_type.2: Shallow or reduced tillage minimises soil inversion and preserves most of the soil biology and structure, a significant improvement over full tillage.
tillage_type.3: No tillage keeps the soil food web fully intact, maintains mycorrhizal networks, locks in organic matter and protects the soil surface year-round — the foundation of regenerative soil management.

residue_cover.1: Less than 15% residue cover signals intensive tillage with minimal residue retention. Exposed soil is vulnerable to erosion, moisture loss and biological depletion.
residue_cover.2: 15–30% residue cover indicates reduced tillage and meaningful retention, protecting soil from erosion and beginning to return organic matter to the surface.
residue_cover.3: More than 30% residue cover — the conservation tillage benchmark — protects the soil surface, feeds soil organisms and significantly increases organic matter over time.

soil_cover_pct.1: Less than 40% soil cover means significant bare soil periods — leaving soil exposed to erosion, UV degradation and moisture loss, while starving soil organisms of organic inputs.
soil_cover_pct.2: 40–60% cover represents a meaningful transition step: erosion is reduced and soil biology is beginning to be supported, moving toward the regenerative threshold.
soil_cover_pct.3: 60% or more cover — the Rainforest Alliance regenerative certification threshold — means the soil is protected, fed and biologically active throughout the year.

intercropping.1: Monoculture creates uniform conditions that favour pest and disease outbreaks, requires high external inputs and provides no resilience if market conditions change.
intercropping.2: Partial diversification with cover crops or alley cropping begins to introduce ecological interactions that can suppress pests, fix nitrogen and improve soil health.
intercropping.3: Intercropping or alley cropping with diverse species including legumes maximises ecological interactions, nitrogen fixation, habitat provision and soil cover — all at once.

synthetic_use.1: Regular synthetic inputs — fertilisers, herbicides and pesticides — destroy soil biology, create resistance, contaminate groundwater and make the farm dependent on external supply chains.
synthetic_use.2: Reducing synthetics and introducing organic alternatives begins to rebuild soil biology and reduce contamination, though the transition period requires careful management.
synthetic_use.3: Fully organic practice eliminates soil biology disruption, contamination and supply chain dependency, building long-term fertility and biological pest management.

plastic_mulch.1: Standard plastic mulch accumulates in soil as microplastics over time, contributing to soil and water contamination, and has no pathway to regenerative certification.
plastic_mulch.2: Biodegradable or paper-based mulch reduces contamination risk. Thick plastic (≥2mm) extends service life and improves recyclability compared to thin single-use films.
plastic_mulch.3: Organic mulching with pruning waste, cover crops or biomass eliminates plastic use entirely, returns organic matter to the soil and supports soil biology.

infrastructure_plastic.1: PVC-based irrigation pipes and protective textiles contain harmful additives, are difficult to recycle and represent long-term contamination risk when degraded in the field.
infrastructure_plastic.2: Reducing PVC in infrastructure reduces contamination risk; transitioning to single-polymer systems improves recyclability at end of life.
infrastructure_plastic.3: Eliminating PVC from irrigation and using organic or bio-based tree supports removes contamination risk and demonstrates alignment with circular economy principles.

waste_disposal.1: Burning agricultural plastics releases toxic dioxins and furans. Leaving them in fields causes progressive microplastic contamination of soil and groundwater.
waste_disposal.2: Municipal waste disposal is the legal minimum but typically lacks the traceability and professional handling needed for proper agricultural plastic recycling.
waste_disposal.3: Participation in a certified scheme like MAPLA ensures proper recycling, documented traceability, polymer separation and compliance with EU agricultural plastic regulations.

knowledge_exchange.1: Without peer learning and knowledge exchange, farming practices evolve slowly while science and best practice advance rapidly — missing opportunities for improvement and connection.
knowledge_exchange.2: Participating a few times per year in events or informal networks provides exposure to new ideas, peer comparison and the motivation that comes from shared purpose.
knowledge_exchange.3: Active participation in farmer networks, field days and peer learning at 3–5+ events per year accelerates knowledge transfer, builds relationships and can catalyse rapid practice change.

community_identity.1: Operating without a sense of community connection makes it harder to access shared resources, collective knowledge and the social support that sustains long-term regenerative commitment.
community_identity.2: Feeling connected to the region is a positive foundation, but translating that into active community investment multiplies the impact for both the farm and the territory.
community_identity.3: A farmer embedded in the local community and invested in Valencia's collective identity is more likely to contribute to, and benefit from, the regional regenerative agriculture movement.

local_decisions.1: Non-participation in local decision-making means the farmer has no voice in water allocation, land use planning or policy that directly affects farming conditions.
local_decisions.2: Attending meetings passively provides awareness of decisions and relationships, but active participation creates real influence over outcomes.
local_decisions.3: Active participation in horizontal decision-making builds collective governance capacity, shapes policy and demonstrates the social dimension of regenerative agriculture.

cooperative_networks.1: Without cooperative membership, the farmer lacks access to collective bargaining, shared services, grant applications and the technical support that networks provide.
cooperative_networks.2: Cooperative membership opens access to resources and networks, though active engagement is needed to convert membership into real benefit.
cooperative_networks.3: Active cooperative membership provides access to grants, policy connections, market opportunities and peer support — multiplying the resources available for regenerative transition.

soil_structure.1: Hard, blocky, compacted soil indicates poor biological activity, high tillage intensity and low organic matter — water infiltration and root development are both severely limited.
soil_structure.2: Moderately loose soil with some aggregates shows biological activity is recovering. This is a transitional state that will improve with continued cover and reduced tillage.
soil_structure.3: Loose, spongy soil with rounded small aggregates is the signature of a biologically active soil — produced by earthworms, fungi and root activity. This is the regenerative goal.

soil_colour.1: Pale, grey or yellowish soil indicates very low organic matter content — biological activity is minimal and the soil has limited ability to store water, nutrients or carbon.
soil_colour.2: Medium-coloured soil indicates average organic matter levels — a good baseline from which regenerative practices can build.
soil_colour.3: Dark brown or black soil indicates high organic matter content and biological richness — carbon is being stored, water is retained and soil life is thriving.

soil_smell.1: No smell or an artificial/chemical odour from soil indicates very low biological activity or contamination from synthetic inputs — microbial life is essentially absent.
soil_smell.2: A faint earthy smell indicates moderate microbial activity. Geosmin — the compound behind the earthy smell — is produced by Actinobacteria, a good sign of developing soil life.
soil_smell.3: A strong, fresh forest smell (petrichor) indicates abundant mycorrhizal fungi and actinobacterial communities — the hallmark of a fully biologically active, healthy soil ecosystem.

root_content.1: Few or no roots in the top 20 cm indicates poor root development, likely due to compaction, low organic matter or biological soil health problems limiting plant growth.
root_content.2: An appreciable number of roots indicates moderate root development and a soil structure permeable enough for plant roots to explore — a positive sign of improving conditions.
root_content.3: Abundant roots and rootlets throughout the soil profile indicate excellent soil porosity, biological activity and plant health — roots are the primary input of organic matter to soil.
